serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu is approximately 300 g, comprising bone-in chicken pieces in a rich spiced gravy, usually served alongside rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, prepared — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken curry (FDC ID 173720) for cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides the closest approved match for a South Indian chicken curry with freshly roasted spices. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN 'Chicken curry' entry (approximately 130 kcal/100 g, protein ~11 g, fat ~8 g, carbohydrate ~4 g per 100 g) and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy chicken curry data. Kongunadu-style preparation uses dry-roasted whole spices and coconut, which slightly elevates fat and energy relative to a generic curry; values adjusted upward modestly to reflect this. No exact Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu entry exists in any approved primary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on 'Chicken curry' GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous South Indian chicken curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 30–45 for meat-based curries with low carbohydrate content. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are onion, tomato, and coconut, all low-GI ingredients. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 13.5) / 100 ≈ 4.7 g (serving size 300 g, carbohydrate 4.5 g/100 g × 3 = 13.5 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100 g; ~1140 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single restaurant serving of Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu may contribute approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing roughly 50% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu has a low estimated GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.7 per restaurant serving, making it a suitable protein-rich, low-glycaemic option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or parotta.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) and sodium (~380 mg/100 g) from coconut and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut and oil content in Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu contributes moderate saturated fat (~8.4 g per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose preparations with less coconut, and pair with vegetables rather than refined carbohydrates to manage overall dietary fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu is a traditional chicken curry originating from the Kongunadu region of western Tamil Nadu, India, encompassing cities such as Coimbatore, Erode, and Tiruppur. The dish is distinguished by its use of freshly dry-roasted whole spices — including coriander seeds, cumin, black pepper, and dried red chillies — which are ground into a coarse masala paste immediately before cooking, imparting a deeply aromatic, robust, and slightly smoky flavour profile that sets it apart from generic South Indian chicken curries. The gravy base is built from sautéed onions, tomatoes, ginger, and garlic, enriched with freshly grated or ground coconut, and finished with curry leaves and turmeric. The chicken, typically bone-in pieces, is slow-cooked in this masala until the oil separates and the gravy thickens to a semi-dry, clinging consistency. The result is a rich, dark-red curry with complex layered heat, earthy spice notes, and a subtle coconut sweetness. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 142 kcal, 11.5 g protein, 9.2 g fat, and 4.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate preparation. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 35 (low), and a standard 300 g restaurant serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 4.7, making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high (~380 mg/100 g), warranting attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is typically served with steamed rice, idiyappam (string hoppers), or parotta in Tamil Nadu, and is a staple of Kongunadu home cooking and regional restaurants.

ingredient_synonyms.kongunadu: Kongu Nadu — western Tamil Nadu region

confidence_reason: No exact Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu entry exists in any approved primary source (ICMR-NIN, USDA, HPB). Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN 'Chicken curry' entry and cross-validated with USDA SR Legacy data, with a modest upward adjustment for coconut and dry-roasted spice content characteristic of Kongunadu preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for comparable South Indian chicken curry dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods source for chicken curry preparations. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for chicken curry. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in non-hydrogenated oil preparations per ICMR-NIN and USDA data — confirmed zero for this preparation type. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ≈ 35, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for South Indian chicken curry; no direct GI measurement for Kongunadu Kozhi Kuzhambu was found in the approved literature. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the dish being a chicken preparation without pork or alcohol; kosher status is not confirmed. 7. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home preparations may have lower sodium. 8. The restaurant serving this dish on field survey data is 'Manam - Upper Circular Road'; nutrient values are based on approved nutritional databases, not restaurant-specific analysis.
